topic: Area D: The World of Work
subject: french

items.q: When do you use the imperfect rather than the passé composé?
items.correct: For ongoing, habitual, or descriptive past actions.
items.random_wrong: When you don't know what tense to use.
items.why: Students confuse past tenses by recency. The distinction is ASPECTUAL: passé composé = completed event ('I went'); imparfait = ongoing/habitual/descriptive ('I used to go', 'I was going').

items.q: How do you say 'I study maths' in French?
items.why: Students drop the article like English. School subjects in French take the DEFINITE article: 'j'aime le français', 'j'étudie les sciences', 'je déteste l'histoire'. This is the rule for general nouns.

items.q: What tense follows 'depuis' for ongoing situations in French?
items.correct: Present tense (J'habite ici DEPUIS 5 ans - I have lived here for 5 years).
items.misconception: Past tense (because it is translated as 'have lived' in English).
items.why: Students translate the English tense literally. French uses PRESENT with depuis because the action is ongoing - 'I am still living'. The English perfect tense misleads students into using past in French.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I would like to be a doctor'?
items.why: It's J'aimerais être médecin.. “J'aime être un médecin.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: an internship. “a salary” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many endings does the French simple future tense have?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 endings. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
